['Make sure to use the recommended nozzle and heatbed temperatures. If you are not sure if the height is correct, open the thumb screws on the side of the heatbreak, and push the nozzle up. Tighten the thumb screws just by hand, do not use any tools.', '1. The video features our Original Prusa MK3, but the same principles of heating the filament blob in order to soften it and carefully removing it with pliers\xa0**apply to all our FDM printer models', 'Preheat error

response: To preheat the nozzle on FDM printer models, go to the LCD Menu -> Settings/Control -> Temperature -> Nozzle and set the temperature approximately 30°C higher than the temperature at which the blob has formed.
reference: To preheat the nozzle on FDM printer models, go to the LCD Menu, then select Settings/Control, followed by Temperature, and finally choose Nozzle.

user_input: What mean if heater cable broken?
retrieved_contexts:
['It is a\xa0**safety feature**\xa0that prevents the printer from overheating in case of a faulty thermistor because, by default, the printer is set to balance out any heat loss by pumping more power into the heat bed or hotend heater cartridge.', 'On single-tool XL, after\xa0**removing the x-carriage cover**, hold the heater block\xa0**(make sure that the nozzle is not heated!)**, and\xa0**use the Torx TX8 key**\xa0as shown in the picture to tighten the set screw', 'In case there is visible damage on the cable, or if it behaves erratically, the thermistor must be replaced. You can follow our guides\xa0[How to replace a heatbed thermistor (MK3S+/MK3S/MK2.5S/MK2S)](http://help.prusa3d', 'Heatbed Tiles', 'And to compensate for the internal stresses and cooling effects of the filament', 'Temperature', 'Heat bed thermistor', 'Enough settings, pass me my heat‑gun!', 'Do not leave the heat source on the printed model for longer than one or two seconds, as this may deform the part.', 'Thermistor connection', 'Hotend thermistor

At the other end of the hotend thermistor cable, inspect the wire where it goes into the heater block. This point is fragile and can get damaged.', 'You can also test it with\xa0**a multimeter**\xa0(our article\xa0[Multimeter usage](http://help.prusa3d.com/article/multimeter-usage_2117)\xa0can guide you). If it fails to read the resistance on the metal inserts in the jumper connectors, then the wire is broken', 'What does it look like?

When one of the thermistor cables is broken, it can be observed when the temperature on the LCD screen starts randomly jumping up and down during the Y-axis or X-axis movement.', 'It is a\xa0**safety feature**\xa0that prevents the printer from overheating in case of a faulty thermistor because, by default, the printer is set to balance out any heat loss by pumping more power into the heat bed or hotend heater cartridge.', 'In case there is visible damage on the cable, or if it behaves erratically, the thermistor must be replaced. You can follow our guides\xa0[How to replace a heatbed thermistor (MK3S+/MK3S/MK2.5S/MK2S)](http://help.prusa3d', "Also, printing in very cold environments, outside of the recommended operational temperature, can have negative effects due to other factors than the printer's electronics, like material properties.", 'Do not leave the heat source on the printed model for longer than one or two seconds, as this may deform the part.', 'If the cable regains a proper connection and a correct reading from the thermistor, the printer will update the error to "MINTEMP fixed" or "MINTEMP fixed", followed by "Please restart".\xa0**This does not mean the issue is permanently resolved**', 'Hotend

Stringing in the nozzle might be caused by a heat dissipation issue in the nozzle. Try re‑applying thermal paste on the threads between the heatbreak and the heatsink.', 'Again, be very cautious around the leads coming from the hot end, in particular, the thermistor leads']
response: A broken heater cable can cause the temperature on the LCD screen to randomly jump up and down during movement. If the cable is broken, the thermistor must be replaced.
reference: If heater cable is broken, you will see a Preheat error message on the LCD.

We recommend using a separating agent (e.g. a glue stick)', 'Glue', 'If your first layer is showing one side higher than the others on the Original Prusa XL, check the assembly of the Core for any loose screws which hold the Core to the four extrusions', 'To achieve the best adhesion of the print surface, it is vital to keep it clean.\xa0**The best option**\xa0when printing with ABS, PLA, and many other materials is\xa0**90% Isopropyl alcohol,**\xa0which can usually be sourced locally at drugstores or hardware stores', 'PEI can lose its adhesive powers after a couple of hundred hours. When you see models coming loose regularly, wipe the surface thoroughly with\xa0**acetone**\xa0to restore the adhesion']
response: Common first layer issues include nozzle conditions, expansion joints, layer shifting, and uneven first layers. To ensure proper first layer adhesion, use a clean bed, correct Z-offset, and consider using adhesives like glue stick, brim option in PrusaSlicer, or a separating agent for certain filaments.
reference: Common first layer issues include poor adhesion, which can lead to print failures. To ensure proper first layer adhesion, regularly maintain the print surface, clean the bed, and set the correct Z-offset. Additionally, using adhesives like glue stick can help improve adhesion. Addressing these factors can significantly reduce the chance of encountering first layer problems.

If the issue persists, test the thermistor with a multimeter to check for resistance; it should read between 80 kΩ and 125 kΩ at room temperature. If the thermistor cable is broken, you may observe temperature fluctuations on the LCD screen during movement, indicating a need for replacement.

response:
To check the belt tension on your Original Prusa MK3, remove the front cover and ensure the belt clamps are in good visible status, then follow the guidelines in the dedicated article on adjusting belt tension. You can also use the belt tuner or measure the distance between the beginning of the motor shaft and the pulley to check the tension.

To address an extruder blob issue on your 3D printer, carefully heat the nozzle to soften the blob, remove material with pliers or tweezers, and inspect the hotend for damage before resuming prints.
reference: To check the belt tension on your Original Prusa MK3, you can use the belt tuner for a quick assessment, following the instructions in the provided video. Alternatively, you can check the Belt Status numbers via the LCD Menu under Support. Ideally, the values should be around 250 for the X-axis and 275 for the Y-axis, with a tolerance of ±15. If the value is under 240, you need to loosen the belt; if it's over 290, you need to tighten it. For addressing an extruder blob, preheat the nozzle to approximately 30°C higher than the temperature at which the blob formed (e.g., 250°C for PLA). After preheating, carefully remove the softened plastic with pliers, ensuring to be cautious around the thermistor leads. If necessary, use a heat gun to assist in softening the blob.

response: Filament moisture can cause stringing issues in 3D printing by affecting the filament's viscosity and internal stresses, leading to a higher likelihood of filament sticking to the print surface. To mitigate this problem, you can try using a different spool of filament, lowering the nozzle temperature by 5-10°C, or adjusting settings such as avoiding crossing perimeters, using sequential printing, or lowering the retraction length.
reference: Filament moisture can significantly affect stringing issues in 3D printing. If the filament has gathered moisture, it can lead to excessive stringing during the printing process. To mitigate this problem, it is recommended to try using a different spool of filament that is dry. Additionally, lowering the nozzle temperature by 5–10 °C can help decrease the occurrence of strings. Proper retraction settings in PrusaSlicer should also be configured, as they play a crucial role in reducing stringing. Ensuring that the nozzle is clean and free from remnants of previous filaments is also essential for optimal printing results.

retrieved_contexts:
['We prepared a short video on how to remove an extruder blob. The video features our Original Prusa MK3, but the same principles of heating the filament blob in order to soften it and carefully removing it with pliers\xa0**apply to all our FDM printer models', 'If your first layer is showing one side higher than the others on the Original Prusa XL, check the assembly of the Core for any loose screws which hold the Core to the four extrusions', 'Original Prusa MK-series', '6. In case you do not succeed, as well as in case the blob has damaged some of the extruder parts, you can find your spare parts\xa0[here](https://shop.prusa3d.com/en/18-spare-parts)', 'Another instance of obstruction is when the zip ties, or other parts of the extruder cable bundle, are not optimally positioned as indicated in their respective\xa0[assembly guide](http://help.prusa3d.com/attachment/ltlznibfugnpwtwy-jpg_272)', '**Please see our dedicated article\xa0[Clogged nozzle/hotend (MK3.5/S, MK3S+, MK2.5S)](http://help.prusa3d.com/article/clogged-nozzle-hotend-mk3-5-s-mk3s-mk2-5s_2008).**', '**The extruder could be clogged**', 'Fix

- Carefully heat the nozzle to soften the blob.
- Remove material with pliers or tweezers.
- Inspect hotend for damage before resuming prints.', 'Silicone sock

Consider installing a\xa0silicone sock. This is\xa0available in our shop\xa0for\xa0**MK4/S**,\xa0**MK3.9/S**, and\xa0**XL**. Although the component does not prevent extruder blobs, in case this occurs, it makes it easier to remove it.', 'When using this feature, PrusaSlicer will warn you of any possibility of the extruder colliding with an already printed part, but follow the print closely', 'Check the XY axes movement and the belt tension', 'Make sure\xa0**nothing is blocking the movement of your axis**\xa0– Check for any obstructions in the path of the bearings or any possible waste from previous printings stuck around the belt (usually around the Y-axis pulley).', 'Original Prusa MK-series', 'prusa3d.com/article/first-layer-calibration-i3_112364).**', 'The set-screw for the Y-axis is easily accessed, being under the bed, but the X-axis is a bit more tricky. You can access the set-screw for the X-axis through a hole on the top of the extruder (picture below)', 'We highly recommend using our official (factory default) preset settings in PrusaSlicer. However, if you are printing with your own settings, make sure that you have the retraction settings configured correctly.', 'Test movements both from the printer menu (_LCD Menu -> Control -> Move Axis_), and with the printer cold and off, by hand. When testing the movements by hand, test both movements\xa0**along the X and Y axes**, and also\xa0**along both diagonals**.', '5, MK2.5S)](http://help.prusa3d.com/article/mini-rambo-electronics-wiring-mk2s-mk2-5-mk2-5s_2109)\xa0or\xa0[Einsy RAMBo electronics wiring (MK3/MK3S/MK3S+)](http://help.prusa3d', 'Access Settings -> Move Axis -> Move X, on the LCD-menu, and rotate the knob to spin the motor.', '- loose belts
- bent Z-axis rods
- irregularity in your z-axis lead screw at that height', 'Free movement of the axis

Make sure the print head (the part moving along the X-axis) can move freely in the entire range. Especially the cable bundle attached to the extruder should not prevent the print head from reaching the end of the axis.', '**\xa0It is usually associated with an abnormal movement of the X-axis and/or the Y-axis, leading the extruder head to be misaligned mid-printing.', '6. When testing the movements by hand, test both movements\xa0**along the X and Y axes**, and also\xa0**along both diagonals**.', 'The set-screw for the Y-axis is easily accessed, being under the bed, but the X-axis is a bit more tricky. You can access the set-screw for the X-axis through a hole on the top of the extruder (picture below)', 'Extruder blob

An extruder blob, also called a “blob of doom,” is a large accumulation of filament around the nozzle and hotend. It happens when a print detaches from the bed and sticks to the nozzle instead.', 'Another instance of obstruction is when the zip ties, or other parts of the extruder cable bundle, are not optimally positioned as indicated in their respective\xa0[assembly guide](http://help.prusa3d.com/attachment/ltlznibfugnpwtwy-jpg_272)', 'Use the following technique to test if the pulley is correctly tightened and if the belt is not too loose. Hold the X-axis motor shaft with pliers (take advantage of the flat part of the shaft) and try to move with the extruder', 'If the steps described above didn’t help, then try decreasing the printing speed. The easiest way how to do it is by rotating the knob during the printing process, lowering the percentage']
response:
To troubleshoot an extruder blob issue on the Original Prusa MK3, carefully heat the nozzle to soften the blob and remove it with pliers or tweezers. If the blob has damaged some extruder parts, you can find spare parts on the Prusa 3D shop website.

Extruder blob issues can affect the movement of the X/Y axes by causing the extruder head to be misaligned mid-printing, leading to improper movement.
reference: To troubleshoot an extruder blob issue on the Original Prusa MK3, first preheat the nozzle to approximately 30°C higher than the temperature at which the blob formed. For example, preheat to 250°C for PLA or 280°C for ABS/PETG. After the hotend reaches the right temperature, leave it for at least 5 minutes, then carefully remove the softened plastic with pliers. To prevent blobs, ensure the print surface is clean and the first layer height is properly adjusted. Additionally, for proper movement of the X/Y axes, check that the belts are tightened, the pulleys are aligned, and there are no obstructions in the path of the bearings. This ensures that the print head can move freely, which is crucial for preventing issues like extruder blobs.

response:
To check belt tension and ensure proper motor pulley alignment on both the Original Prusa MK-series and Original Prusa XL printers, follow these steps:

For belt tension: Remove the front cover, check the belt clamps for visual damage, and use the guidelines in the dedicated belt adjustment guide to adjust the tension. Ensure that the belt clamps are in good visible status and that there is no slack from the belt or any axis movement during the procedure.

For motor pulley alignment: Check that the pulley is flush with the top edge of the motor shaft, and that the set screws are aligned with the flat part of the motor shaft.
reference: For the Original Prusa MK-series, to check the belt tension, you can use the belt tuner or check the Belt Status numbers via the LCD Menu under Support. The optimal tension values are approximately 250 on the X-axis and 275 on the Y-axis, with a tolerance of ±15. If the value is below 240, the belt needs to be loosened, and if it's above 290, it needs to be tightened. Additionally, ensure that the X and Y motors are securely tightened in their mounts and that the pulleys are aligned and can move freely. For the Original Prusa XL, check the belt tension using the guidelines in the dedicated article and inspect the belt clamps for any visible damage. If a motor pulley has loosened, it can cause misalignment, so ensure that the pulleys are positioned correctly: the left pulley should be 2.5mm higher than the flat part of the motor shaft, while the right pulley should be flush with the top edge of the motor shaft.

response: To prevent extruder blobs on the Original Prusa MK3, check the assembly of the extruder cable bundle for optimal positioning and ensure the hotend is properly cleaned and maintained. Installing a silicone sock can also make it easier to remove blobs if they occur.
reference: To prevent extruder blobs while using the Original Prusa MK3, you should follow these steps: First, always clean the print surface before starting the print to ensure proper adhesion. Second, adjust the first layer height at the beginning of the print to make sure that the first layer sticks properly to the entire print surface. Additionally, monitor the print periodically; if you notice any potential issues, pause the print to address them. As a last resort, you can stop the print, adjust the settings, and start again. For optimal printer performance, ensure that your printer is running in normal mode rather than stealth mode, as stealth mode disables the crash detection feature. Also, check that the extruder and heatbed can move freely, verify that the smooth rods are clean and lubricated, and ensure that the X/Y axis motors and pulleys are properly tightened and aligned.

If the issue persists, you can test the thermistor by using a hairdryer to blow hot air at the heatbed or hotend and check for any temperature change on the LCD screen. A positive temperature change indicates that the thermistor is working, suggesting an issue with the heater or heatbed. If there is no change, the thermistor may be defective.

To adjust the printing speed for optimal results, decrease the printing speed, especially when printing large objects, and consider checking your printer's speed in the LCD menu under Tune -> Speed.
reference: To ensure the printing bed is correctly prepared, make sure that the print plate is properly installed, with no leftovers from previous prints or loose filament affecting its position. The sheet should be straight and not bent or damaged. If the print surface is uneven, you can temporarily add a piece of paper under the uneven area or perform firmware Bed Level Correction for a more permanent solution. Regarding printing speed, if you encounter issues, try decreasing the printing speed to about 75% for the first three layers by rotating the knob counterclockwise during the printing process. After the initial layers, you can return the speed to normal.
